sections.headline: The Death of John the Baptist

verses.text: At that time, Herod the tetrarch heard the news about Jesus
and said to his servants, “This is John the Baptist. He has risen from the dead, and that is why these miraculous powers are at work in him.”
For Herod had arrested John, bound him, and put him in prison because of Herodias, his brother Philip's wife,
because John had kept telling him, “It is not lawful for you to have her.”
Although he wanted to kill him, he feared the people, for they considered John a prophet.
But on Herod's birthday, the daughter of Herodias danced before the company and pleased Herod,
so he promised with an oath to give her whatever she wished.
At her mother's urging, she said, “Give me the head of John the Baptist here on a platter.”
Although the king was distressed, because of his oaths and guests, he ordered it to be given.
He sent and had John beheaded in prison.
His head was brought on a platter, given to the girl, and she took it to her mother.
John's disciples came, took the body, buried it, then went and reported to Jesus.
When Jesus heard this, he withdrew by boat to a solitary place, but the crowds, hearing of it, followed him on foot from the towns.
When he came ashore, he saw a great crowd, felt compassion for them, and healed their sick.
As evening approached, the disciples came to him and said, “This is a remote place, and the hour is late. Send the crowds away to the villages to buy food for themselves.”
But Jesus said, “They do not need to go away. You give them something to eat.”
They said, “We have only five loaves and two fish.”
He said, “Bring them here to me.”
He instructed the crowds to sit on the grass. Taking the five loaves and two fish, he looked up to heaven, blessed them, broke them, and gave them to the disciples, who shared them with the crowds.
They all ate and were satisfied, and the disciples gathered twelve full baskets of leftovers.
Those who ate were about five thousand men, besides women and children.
Immediately, Jesus made his disciples get into the boat and go ahead of him to the other side, while he dismissed the crowds.
Right after he went up on the mountain by himself to pray. When evening came, he was there alone.
Meanwhile, the boat was already in the middle of the sea, battered by the waves, for the wind was against it.
In the fourth watch of the night, Jesus came to them, walking on the sea.
When the disciples saw him walking on the sea, they were terrified and said, “It is a ghost!” and cried out in fear.
But immediately Jesus said to them, “Take heart! It is I. Do not be afraid.”
Peter answered, “Lord, if it is you, command me to come to you on the water.”
“Come,” he said. Then Peter got down out of the boat, walked on the water, and came toward Jesus.
But when he noticed the strong wind, he was afraid and, beginning to sink, cried out, “Lord, save me!”
Immediately, Jesus reached out his hand, took hold of him, and said, “You of little faith, why did you doubt?”
And when they climbed into the boat, the wind ceased.
Then those in the boat came and worshiped him, saying, “Truly, you are the Son of God.”
When they had crossed over, they came to the region of Gennesaret.
When the people of that place recognized him, they sent word throughout the surrounding region and brought to him all who were sick.
They begged him to let them touch just the edge of his cloak, and all who touched it were healed.

footnotes.content: John's rebuke comes from the Torah, specifically Leviticus 18:16 and 20:21, where taking a brother's wife is forbidden. Herod's act was seen as both adulterous and unlawful by Jewish standards.
The daughter of Herodias is unnamed in the Gospels, but known from Josephus as Salome. Her dance, likely suggestive in nature, exemplifies the decadence of Herod's court.
The Greek verb anachōreō means to withdraw, often for solitude or prayer. Jesus likely sought quiet after hearing of John's death.
“Was moved with compassion” translates the verb esplagchnisthē, expressing deep, gut-level divine mercy—not mere pity.
Jesus' actions—blessing, breaking, giving—foreshadow the Last Supper. The parallel verbs link daily provision with sacrificial meaning.
The word “men” is from the Greek andres, meaning adult males. The crowd total, including women and children, likely exceeded 10,000.
The Greek verb ēnankasen means 'to compel' or 'to strongly urge.' “Urgently” matches the forcefulness of the moment better than “immediately”.
“Fourth watch” refers to 3–6 a.m., the last Roman division of the night—emphasizing exhaustion and divine timing.
“It is I” translates egō eimi, which can bear divine overtones. Here, it provides assurance rather than a theophanic claim.
The verb katapontizesthai means to sink deeply—used here to portray Peter's physical and spiritual faltering.
“Fringe of his garment” refers to the tzitzit, or tassels commanded in Numbers 15:38. The Greek verb diasōthēsan means to be delivered, healed, or made whole.
